text.title: Annotation Rules and Modern Syntax
text.summary: Built-in generics, PEP 695 type parameter syntax, union rules, TypedDict, and Protocol patterns for Python 3.10+.

rule.statement: Use built-in generic forms: `list[int]`, `dict[str, int]`, `set[str]`, `tuple[int, ...]`, `type[T]`. Never import `List`, `Dict`, `Set`, `Tuple`, `Type` from `typing`.
rule.rationale: PEP 585 (Python 3.9+) made built-in types subscriptable. The `typing` module aliases are deprecated and will be removed. ruff rule UP006 auto-fixes them with `--fix`. Removing them reduces import noise.

rule.statement: Use `X | None` for nullable types and `X | Y` for unions. Never use `Optional[X]` or `Union[X, Y]` in new code on Python 3.10+.
rule.rationale: PEP 604 pipe syntax reads as English "X or None". `Optional[X]` hides the nullable nature behind a name; `X | None` is explicit. ruff UP007 auto-upgrades `Optional` to the pipe form.

rule.statement: On Python 3.12+ floor, use PEP 695 type parameter syntax (`class Repo[T]:`, `def first[T](items: list[T]) -> T:`) instead of `TypeVar`/`Generic` boilerplate.
rule.rationale: PEP 695 eliminates the `T = TypeVar('T')` declaration, reduces noise, and integrates with `type` alias statements. The old form still works but is visually heavier and requires separate import management.

example.code:
# Python 3.12+ — no TypeVar import needed class Repository[T]: def get(self, id: int) -> T | None: ... def list(self) -> list[T]: ... def create(self, item: T) -> T: ...
def first[T](items: list[T]) -> T | None: return items[0] if items else None
# Type alias with PEP 695 type JSON = dict[str, "JSON"] | list["JSON"] | str | int | float | bool | None type Callback[T] = Callable[[T], bool]

example.note: Legacy TypeVar/Generic boilerplate — replaced by PEP 695 on 3.12+
example.code:
from typing import TypeVar, Generic # Unnecessary on 3.12+ T = TypeVar('T')
class Repository(Generic[T]): # Verbose, extra import def get(self, id: int) -> T | None: ...

rule.statement: Use `TypedDict` to type dictionary shapes at cross-boundary points (API responses, config dicts, queue payloads). Use `Required`/`NotRequired` to annotate optional fields explicitly.
rule.rationale: TypedDict is zero-overhead at runtime (it's just a type) and makes shape contracts explicit for dict-heavy code. Unlike Pydantic, it requires no instantiation cost — ideal for annotating existing dict-returning functions without migrating them to dataclasses.

example.note: TypedDict with NotRequired for optional fields
example.code:
from typing import TypedDict, NotRequired, Required
class UserPayload(TypedDict): id: int email: str name: str bio: NotRequired[str] avatar_url: NotRequired[str | None]
# total=False with Required for inversion pattern class PatchPayload(TypedDict, total=False): name: str bio: str avatar_url: str | None # Override: email is always required even in total=False dict email: Required[str]

rule.statement: Use `Protocol` for structural duck-typing contracts instead of ABC inheritance. Reserve ABC for class hierarchies where `super()` call chains are needed.
rule.rationale: Protocol satisfies the Open/Closed principle: any class with the right methods satisfies the Protocol, without importing or subclassing it. ABC requires explicit inheritance, creating import coupling between modules.

rule.statement: Add `@runtime_checkable` only when the Protocol is used in `isinstance()` checks at runtime. Avoid it on Protocols used purely as type annotations — it adds per-call overhead.
rule.rationale: `@runtime_checkable` checks only structural presence of methods, not their signatures. On hot paths, the overhead is measurable. Only add it when `isinstance(obj, MyProtocol)` is needed in production code.

example.note: Protocol for structural typing — no ABC inheritance
